behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition
behaviors.justification: The real failure would be the tool failing to detect renames, counting them as delete+add instead. This depends on two things: (1) the tool invoking git with rename detection flags (like `-M`), and (2) correctly parsing git's rename output format. A Unit test with mocked subprocess could test the parsing logic, but would miss failures in how git is invoked. A mock also cannot guarantee it reflects git's actual rename detection format across versions. Integration testing with a real repository containing a renamed file ensures the tool correctly invokes git with proper flags AND correctly parses the actual output.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository with a file renamed in a recent commit (e.g., old_name.py → new_name.py). Run the tool on this repository and verify: (1) the renamed file appears exactly once in a 'renamed' category, (2) it does not appear in both 'deleted' and 'added' categories, (3) the count of renamed files matches the actual renames in the history.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output
behaviors.justification: The real failure is either a crash when encountering binary files or failure to count them. A Unit test with mocked subprocess could test parsing of the 'Binary files differ' marker, but git's actual output format for binary files can vary by version, configuration, and file type detection heuristics. A mock cannot guarantee it matches git's real behavior. Integration testing with actual binary files (images, compiled objects, etc.) in a real repository ensures the tool handles git's actual output format and doesn't crash under real conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository containing various binary files (e.g., .png, .jpg, .bin, .o object files) that are added, modified, or deleted in the recent history. Run the tool and verify: (1) it does not crash, (2) binary files are correctly counted in the output, (3) the report includes them without attempting to show diffs.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the tool crashing or throwing an error when git commands fail on an empty repository. This is a defensive-coding issue that can be tested at Unit level by mocking subprocess.run to return the error response that git produces (e.g., 'fatal: your current branch has no commits yet'). The tool's error-handling logic can be verified without needing a real repository. A mock of subprocess correctly simulates git's response for this edge case, and any failure in exception handling would be caught.
behaviors.test_description: Mock subprocess.run to return the error or empty-output response that git produces when there are no commits (e.g., empty string or fatal error message). Call the tool's main logic with this mocked git response and verify: (1) no exception is raised, (2) an empty report is produced, (3) the output indicates zero changes in all categories.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests end-to-end correctness of the tool's counting logic against real git repositories. A Unit test with mocked git output could verify individual parsing steps, but would likely miss edge cases that appear in real repositories: merge commits, file mode changes, renames combined with modifications, or subtle differences in how git reports changes. A mock cannot guarantee coverage of all real-world scenarios. Integration testing with actual repositories containing known, varied change histories ensures the reported counts exactly match the actual changes and catches subtle counting bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a series of git repositories, each with a known and distinct set of changes: (1) files added (3 files), (2) files deleted (2 files), (3) files renamed (1 file), (4) files modified (4 files), and (5) a mix of the above. Run the tool on each repository and verify that the counts in the output exactly match the actual number of changes in each category. Repeat with different orderings and combinations to cover edge cases.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the tool producing different outputs when analyzing staged versus unstaged changes in equivalent repository states. This requires verifying the tool correctly invokes both `git diff` (for unstaged) and `git diff --cached` (for staged) and synthesizes them into a consistent report. A Unit test with mocked subprocess could theoretically test this, but would require mocking multiple calls and carefully controlling state, and any error in mock setup could hide the real failure. Integration testing with real repositories in both states ensures the tool correctly reads both git diff modes and produces identical results for equivalent changes.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository with a set of known changes (e.g., 2 files modified, 1 file added, 1 file deleted). Stage all changes and run the tool, recording the output. In a separate repository, make the same changes but leave them unstaged. Run the tool and verify the output is identical to the first run. Repeat this process with multiple distinct sets of changes (including edge cases like renames, binary files, and combinations) to ensure consistency.
